description: REVIEW REQUIRED. LLM-extracted claims and LLM-PROPOSED relationships. Inspect and approve the relationships before running the engine. Reliability is from document metadata, not LLM-judged.

claims.claim_text: The great landmasses of the Earth are not fixed in place but have drifted and continue to drift slowly across the globe over vast geological time.
claims.source: Drifting-continents monograph (1915), dismissed by the geological establishment of the day

claims.claim_text: The continents were once joined together in a single primordial supercontinent that broke apart, with its fragments wandering to their present positions.
claims.source: Drifting-continents monograph (1915), dismissed by the geological establishment of the day

claims.claim_text: The correspondence of opposing coasts, the distribution of fossils, and the matching of geological formations across oceans are evidence of ancient continental displacement rather than coincidence.
claims.source: Drifting-continents monograph (1915), dismissed by the geological establishment of the day

claims.claim_text: The permanence of the continents and ocean basins is a settled principle of geology, and continents do not and cannot wander across the Earth's surface.
claims.source: Authoritative geology textbook, early-20th-century fixist consensus

claims.claim_text: No known force is adequate to propel continental masses horizontally through the rigid rock beneath them.
claims.source: Authoritative geology textbook, early-20th-century fixist consensus

claims.claim_text: The occurrence of the same fossil species on lands now separated by ocean is properly explained by former land bridges that have since foundered beneath the waves.
claims.source: Authoritative geology textbook, early-20th-century fixist consensus

claims.claim_text: The notion of drifting continents is a fanciful speculation unsupported by any sound mechanism.
claims.source: Authoritative geology textbook, early-20th-century fixist consensus

claims.claim_text: The eastern coastline of South America and the western coastline of Africa mirror each other closely, with the bulge of Brazil corresponding to the inward curve of the Gulf of Guinea.
The geometric fit of the South American and African coastlines is too exact and too extensive to be readily dismissed as coincidence, suggesting the two were once joined.
The seed-fern Glossopteris is found in the rocks of South America, Africa, India, and Australia.
Remains of the freshwater reptile Mesosaurus occur on both sides of the South Atlantic.
The presence of the same land-bound and freshwater species on landmasses now divided by broad oceans is difficult to explain unless those landmasses were formerly contiguous.
The mountain belts and distinctive rock strata of the eastern coast of South America align with those of western Africa, matching in age, sequence, and character.
claims.source: Cross-Atlantic rock-strata and glacial-deposit field notes

claims.claim_text: Ancient glacial deposits of the same period are found across the southern continents in positions that make little sense if the continents were always fixed but fall into order if those lands were once assembled together nearer the pole.
claims.source: Cross-Atlantic rock-strata and glacial-deposit field notes

relationships.rationale: The idea of a primordial supercontinent that broke apart and drifted is a direct elaboration and sub-claim supporting the core thesis that continents have drifted across the globe.
Coastline correspondences, fossil distributions, and matching geological formations are cited as direct evidence for the core claim of continental drift.
The physical and biological evidence of ancient displacement supports the sub-claim that continents were once joined and have since wandered.
The permanence of continents and ocean basins directly contradicts the core thesis that continents drift across the globe.
The absence of any known force capable of moving continents horizontally undermines the core claim that continents do drift.
The lack of a known driving mechanism is the primary basis for dismissing continental drift as fanciful speculation without sound mechanical grounding.
Labelling continental drift as fanciful speculation without a sound mechanism is a direct rejection of the core drift thesis.
Land-bridge subsidence as an explanation for shared fossil species directly contradicts the claim that contiguous landmasses (drift) are needed to explain their distribution.
Explaining shared fossils via sunken land bridges removes a key line of evidence for continental drift, contradicting the core drift claim.
The mirroring of the South American and African coastlines is direct observational evidence supporting the core claim that continents have drifted from formerly joined positions.
The geometric match of the two coastlines supports the sub-claim that the continents were once joined in a supercontinent.
The assessment that the fit is too exact to be coincidence reinforces the core claim that South America and Africa were once connected and have since drifted apart.
The precision of the coastline fit supports the supercontinent sub-claim by implying former physical continuity.
The specific geometric detail of the Brazil–Guinea correspondence is the factual basis for the broader conclusion that the fit is too exact to be coincidental.
The distribution of Glossopteris across four now-separated southern continents is direct fossil evidence supporting the core claim of continental drift.
Glossopteris spanning South America, Africa, India, and Australia supports the idea that these landmasses were once assembled in a single supercontinent.
Glossopteris occurring on four widely separated continents is a concrete example underpinning the general claim that shared land-bound species imply former contiguity.
Mesosaurus, a freshwater reptile, found on both sides of the South Atlantic is strong evidence supporting the core claim that these landmasses were once connected and have drifted apart.
The trans-Atlantic distribution of a freshwater reptile supports the supercontinent sub-claim by making ocean-crossing impossible without former land continuity.
Mesosaurus is a specific example of a freshwater species on now ocean-divided landmasses, directly substantiating the general fossil contiguity claim.
The general principle that shared land-bound and freshwater species across oceans implies former contiguity is a key evidentiary pillar for the core continental drift claim.
Former contiguity of landmasses to explain fossil distributions directly supports the supercontinent sub-claim.
A freshwater reptile like Mesosaurus could not cross an ocean even via a land bridge that later foundered, making the land-bridge explanation implausible for this specific case.
Matching mountain belts and rock strata across the South Atlantic is geological evidence directly supporting the core claim that the continents were once joined and have drifted.
Aligned strata matching in age, sequence, and character supports the sub-claim of a formerly unified supercontinent.
Glacial deposits spread across the southern continents in patterns explicable only by their former polar assembly is powerful evidence for the core continental drift thesis.
The coherence of glacial deposit positions when the southern continents are reassembled near a pole supports the supercontinent sub-claim.
The settled principle of continental permanence provides the established-science backdrop that makes drift appear to be fanciful speculation.
